ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Soya Chaap Masala as a prepared dish. FOODON_03315595 (soy-based food product) is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific term for soya chaap as a wheat-gluten-and-soy composite meat analogue in curry has not been minted in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Soya Chaap Masala. A0CKP (meat analogue, plant-based) is the closest defensible code available in the FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Soya Chaap Masala or soya chaap as a food entity. SNOMED CT food coding for Indian regional prepared dishes is sparse; no defensible code was identified.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant or home serving of Soya Chaap Masala is approximately 250 g, comprising 3–4 soya chaap sticks in a generous portion of creamy onion-tomato gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods does not list Soya Chaap Masala as a discrete entry. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for soy-based preparations and cooked legume dishes, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for textured soy protein and wheat gluten (SR Legacy). Saturated fat is estimated from the cream and butter content typical of tikka masala-style gravies. All values are consistent with the input enriched_json record (confidence 0.9) and are treated as estimated composites.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Soy protein and legume-based analogous foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC — Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Soya Chaap Masala in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 42 is estimated by analogy with soy-protein-based foods and cooked legume preparations (GI range 30–50) documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The high protein (13.5 g/100g), dietary fibre (3.2 g/100g), and fat content (9.8 g/100g) from cream and oil further attenuate glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (42 × 21.0) / 100 = 8.8 (using 250 g serving × 8.4 g carbohydrate/100g = 21.0 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (340 mg/100g; ~850 mg per 250 g serving) and saturated fat from cream and butter
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Soya Chaap Masala to occasional consumption, request reduced-salt preparation, substitute cream with low-fat yogurt, and avoid pairing with high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or papads.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~42), high protein (13.5 g/100g), and dietary fibre (3.2 g/100g) supporting attenuated postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Soya Chaap Masala is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (150–200 g), as its low GI, high protein, and fibre content promote glycaemic stability; pair with non-starchy vegetables and avoid refined-flour breads to further limit glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream and butter (~3.8 g/100g) and sodium (340 mg/100g) are cardiovascular risk factors, partially offset by soy isoflavones and dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Soya Chaap Masala should be prepared with minimal cream and butter, using heart-healthy oils (e.g., canola or olive oil), and portion size should be limited to 150 g to manage saturated fat and sodium intake within daily cardiovascular dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Soy isoflavones and ginger in the spice blend may exert mild anti-inflammatory effects
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Soya Chaap Masala may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, but those with gluten sensitivity should avoid it due to the wheat gluten content of the chaap sticks.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soya Chaap Masala is a popular North Indian vegetarian main dish originating from the Punjab region of India. It is made from soya chaap — cylindrical sticks formed from a blend of soybean flour and wheat gluten wrapped around bamboo skewers — which are marinated in spiced yogurt and then either tandoor-grilled, pan-fried, or deep-fried to develop a firm, slightly charred exterior. The prepared chaap sticks are then simmered in a rich tikka masala-style gravy made from pureed tomatoes, sautéed onions, ginger-garlic paste, aromatic whole and ground spices (cumin, coriander, garam masala, Kashmiri chilli), and finished with heavy cream or butter to create a luxurious, mildly spiced, tangy-creamy sauce. The dish has a fibrous, chewy, meat-like texture that absorbs the gravy deeply, making it a highly satisfying meat alternative. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 172 kcal, 13.5 g protein, 9.8 g fat, 8.4 g carbohydrate, 3.2 g dietary fibre, and 340 mg sodium. Its low glycemic index (~42) and high protein and fibre content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. However, the sodium and saturated fat content from cream and butter make it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Soya chaap is a significant source of plant protein and contains soy isoflavones, which may have mild anti-inflammatory properties. It is not suitable for individuals with soy or gluten allergies. Commonly served at lunch or dinner in North Indian restaurants and dhabas, it is typically paired with naan, roti, or rice.
common_user_queries: Is soya chaap good for diabetes?
Can I eat soya chaap masala if I have high blood pressure?
What is soya chaap made of?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for soy-based Indian preparations and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for textured soy protein and wheat gluten, as no discrete ICMR-NIN entry for Soya Chaap Masala exists. The input enriched_json record carried a confidence of 0.9 from an AI-generated pipeline; values are plausible and internally consistent but represent a composite estimate rather than a direct laboratory measurement. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database soy-protein food category. Confidence is therefore rated medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, cholesterol, and moisture data for Soya Chaap Masala as a prepared composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. Saturated fat (3.8 g/100g) is estimated from the cream and butter content typical of tikka masala-style gravies using USDA FoodData Central data for heavy cream and butter. 2. Portions in the input JSON contained four identical duplicate entries (all '1 serving (250g), 250g'); deduplicated to a single canonical serving of 250 g. 3. GI of 42 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Soya Chaap Masala was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Vitamin B12 (0.3 mcg/100g) is present due to the dairy ingredients (yogurt, cream) in the gravy; this is plausible for a dairy-containing vegetarian dish. 5. This dish contains wheat gluten and is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity. 6. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology code for Soya Chaap Masala exists in the catalogues reviewed as of 2024.
